title: Red Wine on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Red wine contains anthocyanins (red-purple pigments), condensed tannins, and tartaric acid. Anthocyanins bond to upholstery immediately through hydrogen bonding and color intensifies with pH changes — alkaline conditions turn wine stains blue-purple and more difficult to remove. The tannin component oxidizes and darkens over time, cross-linking with the fiber polymer.
whyThisWorks: The acidic tannin spotter maintains correct pH (acidic) which keeps anthocyanins in their most removable red form on upholstery. The oxidizer then breaks the chromophore bonds in the anthocyanin molecule. Avoiding alkaline products is critical — ammonia, baking soda, and alkaline detergents permanently darken wine stains by shifting the anthocyanin into its blue form.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No over-wetting — causes water rings and backing damage
No chlorine bleach on colored fabric
Always check care code (W, S, WS, X) before treating
fiberSensitivities: Mixed fiber blends common — treat for the most sensitive fiber present
Cushion foam absorbs moisture — avoid saturation
Test in hidden area before treating visible sections

escalation.when: If red wine stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is red wine on upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
